title: The Commission of Inquiry into the Events at the Refugee Camps in Beirut (Kahan Report)
author_or_org: State of Israel Commission of Inquiry
pages_or_sections: pp. 17-18 and pp. 27-28 of the mirrored PDF text
geographic_scope: Sabra and Shatila camps and surrounding area
strengths: High-value official inquiry source for layered attribution, including direct perpetrator responsibility and Israeli indirect/personal responsibility findings; also gives a low-end death estimate.
limitations: Its mortality estimate is conservative and contested; not a full victim registry; does not solve denominator problems.
notes: The report says the massacre figure is between 700 and 800 and may be closest to reality, while assigning direct responsibility to the Phalangists and indirect/personal responsibility to senior Israeli decision-makers for failure to prevent the massacre.

title: ICRC/Lebanon: Sabra and Chatila
author_or_org: International Committee of the Red Cross
strengths: Strong humanitarian testimony that access was controlled and that women, children, adolescents, and elderly persons were among the dead.
limitations: Provides vivid on-scene evidence but not a complete massacre death total.
notes: The case study says the ICRC was denied access by the Israeli army until 18 September, that hundreds of women, children, adolescents and elderly persons had been killed, and that about 5,000 persons sought refuge at the ICRC delegation.

title: Sabra and Shatila, 16-18 September 1982
strengths: Useful synthesis of chronology, spatial footprint, direct perpetrator attribution, and a high-end civilian death estimate.
limitations: Secondary synthesis rather than a primary death registry.
notes: States that the Israeli army surrounded the camps, closed roads, prevented residents from leaving, and that at least 3,000 civilians were massacred, with figures varying between 2,000 and 4,500.

title: Sabra and Chatila
author_or_org: Sciences Po / Mass Violence and Resistance Research Network
strengths: Useful secondary source because it reports an official-source count with internal breakdown between identified corpses and family burials.
limitations: Secondary synthesis; still not a fully reconciled victim database.
notes: The entry says official sources reached about 2,000 casualties, including 762 identified corpses buried by the Lebanese army or Red Cross and about 1,200 others buried by families and registered with the Red Cross.

title: Population table for Palestinian camps in Lebanon (Table C.1)
strengths: Provides a documented administrative floor for pre-massacre camp population counts.
limitations: Pre-1982 baseline only; does not cover the massacre window, Sabra spillover, or displacement into surrounding blocks.
notes: The table lists Shatila with 8,278 in one 1979/80 count and 5,435 UNRWA-registered refugees.

title: Shatila Refugee Camp
strengths: Useful for showing why prewar administrative camp counts are too low for the massacre footprint by 1982.
limitations: Contextual rather than episode-window specific; broader than the massacre footprint.
notes: Citing Rosemary Sayigh, the entry says about 20,000 people lived in Shatila and the close surrounding area and that the number quickly reached almost 50,000.
